title: Quick check: returning an isolated host to service

questions.prompt: Put the five items of the un-isolation recovery checklist in the right order.
items: Recommended remediations all applied AND verified.
No re-detection within the last hour or so.
SOC has either closed the Incident Report or signed off on release.
The customer / user has been informed.
questions.explanation: Five items, in order. Skip any of them and un-isolation gets risky.

questions.prompt: Un-isolation is "the easiest action in the portal to click and the hardest one to do correctly." Why?
choices: Walking the recovery checklist BEFORE the click is the discipline.

choices.label: Waiting at least 4 hours after isolation before un-isolating, regardless of state.
choices.rationale: Calendar-driven, not signal-driven. The lesson uses the checklist.

choices.label: Getting senior approval for every un-isolation.
choices.rationale: The checklist governs; not every release needs senior approval.

choices.label: Letting the agent un-isolate automatically after 24 hours.
choices.rationale: Not a feature; un-isolate is an explicit portal action.

questions.explanation: The click is fast; the checklist is what makes the click safe. Walk the five items first; the click follows.

questions.prompt: The customer's IT manager pings hard: "How much longer? My ops team is blocked." None of the recovery checks are complete yet. What's the right move?
choices: Customer pressure isn't a checklist item.

choices.label: Release the host; customer pressure overrides the checklist on commercial grounds.
choices.rationale: Releasing under pressure trains the customer that isolation is a brief interruption rather than a containment.

choices.label: Bump to senior immediately, regardless of where the checklist is.
choices.rationale: Bump only when the pressure escalates beyond what tech-level communication can handle.

choices.label: Stop responding to the IT manager until release is ready.
choices.rationale: Silence makes the situation worse. Honest timeline updates are the lesson's call.

questions.explanation: Update the customer honestly on the timeline ("remediation verified, waiting for SOC sign-off, expect release in ~15 minutes") rather than release early. Containment governs the release decision, not volume. Honest timeline updates are what customer pressure gets.

questions.prompt: How long after un-isolation should you monitor for re-detection?
choices: About an hour. A genuinely-resolved incident produces a clean post-release period;

choices.label: Five minutes; if it doesn't re-fire fast, it won't fire later.
choices.rationale: Too short. Re-detections may take 15–60 minutes to surface.

choices.label: 24 hours; full-day quarantine after release.
choices.rationale: Too long for active monitoring.

choices.label: No monitoring needed once the checklist is green.
choices.rationale: Skipping post-release monitoring is the named common failure.

questions.explanation: A not-quite-resolved one re-detects in this window. Walk away after un-isolation and a re-detection lands on the next shift's queue with no context.

questions.prompt: The SOC has held the Incident Report open with specific release conditions you can't yet satisfy. What's the right move?

choices.label: Release per the runbook; the SOC's notes are advisory.
choices.rationale: SOC-specific release conditions override the runbook default. Releasing against them is a unilateral override.

choices.label: Ignore the SOC notes; the runbook is canonical.
choices.rationale: The SOC's notes are the actual guidance for this specific incident.

choices.label: Reply asking them to remove the conditions.
choices.rationale: Asking the analyst to soften their conditions is litigating.

questions.explanation: If release isn't appropriate per their conditions, wait or bump. Don't release on the runbook's default timing. SOC-held incidents have specific conditions for a reason. Read them, satisfy them, or wait.

questions.prompt: You isolated WS-EXAMPLE-OPS-04 two hours ago. Both remediations applied and verified clean. No new detections since isolation. The IT manager pings: "How much longer? Mike's blocked." What do you check before deciding?

choices.label: Release immediately; Mike needs the machine.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex. Haven't walked the checklist. The IT manager's pressure gets a "soon, here's what I'm waiting on" response.

choices.label: Tell the IT manager you can't release until "the security team says it's safe."
choices.rationale: Wrong tone. You are the security team for this customer in this moment. Hiding behind a vague authority weakens your standing.

questions.explanation: (remediations verified, no recent re-detection, SOC sign-off, user informed, PSA up-to-date). Five greens, release; one yellow, communicate the timeline. IT manager pressure is real but isn't a checklist item. Walk the checklist; act on its state.

questions.prompt: Checklist all green. SOC has closed the Incident Report. You un-isolate. Within three minutes, a new EDR detection fires: a different scheduled task on the same host, similar pattern to the one you remediated. What do you do?

choices.label: Approve the new remediation and let the host stay online.
choices.rationale: Re-detection within minutes of release is a containment-and-escalate signal, not a fix-on-the-fly signal.

choices: Re-isolate the host. Escalate to senior.

choices.label: Investigate the new scheduled task yourself before re-isolating.
choices.rationale: Investigation is above the helpdesk ceiling and the time window matters. Re-isolate first, investigate with senior second.

questions.explanation: Document the pattern in the new Incident Report. The first persistence had a friend. Re-isolate, bump, let the senior plus SOC weigh the next move.

questions.prompt: After un-isolation, what should you do regarding the user?
choices: Confirm with the user that the endpoint is working — brief call or message.

choices.label: Don't contact the user; they'll notice the network is back on their own.
choices.rationale: "Forgetting to tell the user the machine is back" is the named common mistake.

choices.label: Have the customer's IT manager call the user instead; you're done.
choices.rationale: You isolated; the close-the-loop call is yours.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours and email a summary.
choices.rationale: Too late. The user's been waiting; immediate confirmation is the call.

questions.explanation: Wraps the comms loop cleanly. 30 seconds of close-the-loop comms means the user trusts the next isolation when it happens.

questions.prompt: What does the lesson say is "worse than not isolating at all"?
choices: Releasing an endpoint back to the network with the threat unresolved.

choices.label: Isolating for too long.
choices.rationale: Long isolation has customer-relationship cost but isn't worse than the threat being back on the network unresolved.

choices.label: Isolating the wrong host.
choices.rationale: A separate failure mode, not the one this lesson names as worse than not isolating at all.

choices.label: Forgetting to call the user before isolating.
choices.rationale: Different lesson's failure mode.

questions.explanation: You've trained the customer that isolation is a brief interruption rather than a containment, and you've handed the attacker their access back. Premature release undoes the containment and damages future credibility. Worse than not isolating in the first place.

questions.prompt: The customer pushes hard to release the host before remediations are complete and escalates to your account manager. What's the right move?
choices: Customer-relationship piece, often senior territory if the push is intense.

choices.label: Release; commercial pressure outranks security recommendations.
choices.rationale: Commercial pressure goes through the senior, not through the isolation itself.

choices.label: Tell the customer that Huntress contractually prohibits early release.
choices.rationale: Not the framing and likely false on the commercial terms.

choices.label: Release the host but immediately re-isolate ten minutes later.
choices.rationale: Half-measure that defeats both goals.

questions.explanation: The isolation itself stands until the threat is addressed; the customer conversation about how that's handled is the senior's place. Action stands; conversation goes up. The senior handles the customer side.
